title: Lesson 381
transcript: Let's talk about scoping and tailoring as it relates to security. Now, when you look at these two terms, they can be used in all kinds of different contexts, but we need to relate it to security here, obviously. And here are pretty good definitions. of scoping and tailoring and how they differ from each other. So if you think about scoping itself, what's in scope? For example, if you're doing an audit, you can't audit everything. So you're going to think about what you're going to include as part of your audit. That would be what we refer to as scoping, right? You're selecting only those controls for example, that apply to what you're looking at. You're not going to look at everything. You're just going to look at those things that really apply. You're scoping your project. You're scoping whether you're doing a vulnerability assessment. Which controls are you going to look at and include as part of your vulnerability assessment? That's what we call scoping. Tailoring, on the other hand, right? You're modifying, for example, based on what your needs are. right so modifying the baseline to become more applicable to align with goals and objectives and you know a perfect way to think of tailoring if you go to a tailor for example right they're not going to throw away your pants because they don't fit they're going to tailor your pants so that they do fit right so they modify things based on what your requirements are and that's the best way to think of what scoping and tailoring is so from a security perspective scoping means okay which security controls are we going to be looking at, for example, and tailoring, modifying your baseline so that you become more applicable to whatever your needs and your project and your requirements and your objectives are. That's what tailor something to your needs. and you scope which means you understand what's in scope what you're going to include as part of that
